     7→## Question 1: Short Definitions

     9→**(a) General semaphore:**
    10→A general semaphore is a synchronization primitive consisting of an integer variable (initialized to any non-negative value) that is accessed only through two atomic operations: `wait` (P), which decrements the value and blocks the calling process if the result would be negative, and `signal` (V), which increments the value and potentially wakes a blocked process.
    11→
    12→**(b) Best fit:**
    13→Best fit is a memory allocation strategy that, when searching for a free block of memory to satisfy an allocation request, selects the smallest free block that is large enough to satisfy the request, thereby minimizing the amount of leftover (wasted) space in the chosen block.
    14→
    15→**(c) Supervisor mode:**
    16→Supervisor mode (also called kernel mode) is a privileged processor execution mode in which the CPU can execute all instructions including privileged instructions and can access all memory and hardware resources, as opposed to user mode where such access is restricted.
    17→
    18→**(d) Race condition:**

    20→
    21→**(e) Multiprogramming:**
    22→Multiprogramming is a technique in which multiple processes are kept in memory simultaneously, allowing the operating system to switch the CPU between them so that the CPU can execute one process while another is waiting (e.g., for I/O), thereby increasing overall system utilization.
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Question 2: Synchronization Using a Monitor
    27→
    28→**Monitor type: Mesa**
    29→
    30→I use a Mesa monitor because it is the most widely used in practice (e.g., Java, pthreads). With Mesa semantics, a `signal` (or `broadcast`) moves the waiting process to the entry queue but the signaler continues; therefore, waiters must re-check conditions in a `while` loop.
    31→
    32→### Monitor Definition
    33→
    34→```
    35→Monitor BoundedBuffer          /* Mesa semantics */
    36→
    37→    /* ---- shared state ---- */
    38→    const int B = BUFFER_SIZE;          /* capacity of buffer */
    39→    struct item buffer[B];
    40→    int numInBuffer = 0;                /* current number of items */
    41→    int in  = 0;                        /* next insertion index */
    42→    int out = 0;                        /* next removal index */
    43→
    44→    condition notFull;                   /* waited on by producers */
    45→    condition notEmpty;                  /* waited on by consumers */
    46→
    47→    /* -------------------------------------------------- */
    48→    entry procedure AddToBuffer(struct item *data, int count)
    49→    /* count is 1 or 2 */
    50→    {
    51→        /* Wait until there is room for ALL count items.
    52→           Use while (Mesa semantics) because another process
    53→           may have consumed the space before we re-enter. */
    54→        while (numInBuffer + count > B)
    55→            wait(notFull);
    56→
    57→        /* Add count items atomically (monitor mutual exclusion
    58→           guarantees no other entry procedure can interleave). */
    59→        for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
    60→            buffer[in] = data[i];
    61→            in = (in + 1) % B;
    62→            numInBuffer++;
    63→        }
    64→
    65→        /* Wake up waiting consumers -- one per item added. */
    66→        for (int i = 0; i < count; i++)
    67→            signal(notEmpty);
    68→    }
    69→
    70→    /* -------------------------------------------------- */
    71→    entry procedure RemoveFromBuffer(struct item *data)
    72→    {
    73→        /* Wait until there is at least one item. */
    74→        while (numInBuffer == 0)
    75→            wait(notEmpty);
    76→
    77→        data[0] = buffer[out];
    78→        out = (out + 1) % B;
    79→        numInBuffer--;
    80→
    81→        /* Wake ALL waiting producers so that any producer
    82→           whose space requirement is now met can proceed.
    83→           broadcast is used (rather than signal) because
    84→           different producers may need 1 or 2 slots; a single
    85→           signal might wake a producer that still cannot

    87→        broadcast(notFull);
    88→    }

    90→End Monitor

   116→- **No interleaving of a producer's 2 items:** The monitor provides mutual exclusion among all entry procedures. While `AddToBuffer` is executing, no other entry procedure can run, so both items are placed into the buffer atomically with respect to all other producers and consumers.
   117→

If the buffer is empty, consumers wait on `notEmpty`; producers can still enter `AddToBuffer` and will `signal(notEmpty)` to wake consumers. The buffer cannot be simultaneously full and empty, so at least one side can always make progress.
   119→
   120→- **No starvation (under FIFO condition-variable scheduling):** With FIFO ordering on the condition variable queues, every waiting consumer is eventually signaled (since producers keep adding items), and every waiting producer is eventually woken by a broadcast (since consumers keep removing items). Because `broadcast(notFull)` wakes all waiting producers, any producer whose space requirement (1 or 2 slots) is satisfied will proceed. Over time, as consumers continue removing items, sufficient space will accumulate for any waiting producer.
   121→
   122→- **Maximum concurrency:** Producers and consumers only block when the buffer is genuinely full or empty, respectively. There is no unnecessary serialization beyond the monitor's inherent mutual exclusion, which is held only during the brief buffer manipulation.
   123→

   138→};
